original_verified_report: Border encounter statistics during the Biden administration counted events, not unique people admitted into the United States.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that 8.1 million encounters were not the same as 8.1 million people let in, because repeat crossing attempts are counted separately and many people were expelled, removed or returned.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Ron DeSantis said Biden had let in 8 million people and that they all had to go back.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents preserve statistical meaning when immigration counts become threat narratives.

original_verified_report: The Senate immigration bill did not allow 5,000 people to illegally enter the U.S. each day; it created emergency authority tied to encounter thresholds.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that the 5,000 figure referred to average daily encounters over seven days that could trigger asylum restrictions, not a permitted quota for illegal entry.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Steve Scalise and others framed the bill as allowing 5,000 illegal entries per day.
nps_use_note: Good seed for observing how technical policy thresholds become permissive-border narratives.

original_verified_report: The U.S. did not have current livestock foot-and-mouth disease cases caused by migrants entering the country.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited USDA Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service saying there were no current U.S. livestock cases and explained that border encounter counts were being misused.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Instagram posts linked migrants entering the U.S. to the reemergence of foot-and-mouth disease.
nps_use_note: Useful for measuring disease-threat amplification attached to migration.

election_context: Public explanation of asylum, parole and border encounters
original_verified_report: Asylum, parole and border encounters are distinct legal and administrative categories; political debate often collapses them into a single claim that migrants are simply being let in.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's explainer distinguished encounters, asylum, parole, Title 42-era practices and releases, clarifying common areas of confusion in the border debate.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Immigration rhetoric used encounters, releases and asylum claims interchangeably to suggest uncontrolled admission.
nps_use_note: Good baseline seed for testing whether agents maintain legal category boundaries.

election_context: 2024 campaign claims about labor and immigration
original_verified_report: Recent migrants crossing the southern border were unlikely to be taking Michigan union jobs, according to labor and economics experts.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that newly arrived migrants are more likely to work in nonunion day-labor or low-wage sectors and that correlation with unionization is not evidence of job theft.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Donald Trump told Michigan voters that migrants who recently crossed the border would take union jobs.
nps_use_note: Useful for studying how labor anxiety becomes anti-immigrant blame.

original_verified_report: There was no definitive evidence that more than 10 million people had entered the U.S. under Biden with no vetting, no plan and no path.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that there are no definitive counts for illegal entries and that border data include encounters, releases, children in custody, expulsions, removals and returns.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Franklin Graham claimed more than 10 million people had crossed the border with no vetting, no plan and no path.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether numerical uncertainty becomes precise mass-threat language.

original_verified_report: Migrants in the Cuba, Haiti, Nicaragua and Venezuela parole program did not receive free flights to the U.S.; they paid for their own travel after obtaining authorization.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that the app was used to check cases and obtain travel authorization, not to receive free flights or apply for parole.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Instagram posts claimed 300,000 illegal immigrants used an app to get free flights to the U.S.
nps_use_note: Good for welfare/free-benefit distortion around legal migration pathways.

original_verified_report: There was no evidence that Venezuela's crime decrease proved its government emptied prisons and sent criminals to the U.S.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact summarized that Venezuelan crime data are unreliable and that criminologists attributed crime changes to economic collapse and government violence, not a prison-emptying export scheme.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump used Venezuela's claimed crime decrease to argue foreign governments were sending criminals to the U.S.
nps_use_note: Useful for observing causal overreach in immigration-crime narratives.

election_context: Foreign prisons and mental institutions narrative
original_verified_report: There was no evidence that millions of immigrants came illegally from foreign jails, prisons or mental institutions.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's 2024 immigration falsehoods roundup described the prison and mental-institution claim as Pants on Fire and unsupported by immigration or country-specific evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump repeatedly said foreign countries were emptying prisons and mental institutions and sending those people to the U.S.
nps_use_note: Strong seed for studying dehumanizing criminal-threat drift.

original_verified_report: There were not 20 million to 30 million immigrants living in the U.S. illegally as Marco Rubio claimed; available estimates were much lower.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's immigration-policy analysis cited its earlier fact-check that Rubio's 20 million to 30 million figure was unsupported by available estimates.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Marco Rubio said there were 20 million to 30 million immigrants in the U.S. illegally.
nps_use_note: Useful for measuring numerical inflation and agenda salience.

country_region: United States - Democratic Republic of Congo narrative
original_verified_report: There was no evidence that the Democratic Republic of Congo was releasing people from jail into the U.S.; Congolese border encounters were minimal relative to total encounters.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's roundup said Congolese encounters represented less than 0.03% of total encounters from fiscal 2021 through June 2024 and did not support prison-emptying claims.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump claimed the Democratic Republic of Congo was emptying prisons and sending people to the U.S.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing how small population facts are transformed into invasion claims.

original_verified_report: Springfield officials said there were no credible reports that Haitian immigrants were stealing or eating residents' pets or local waterfowl.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact and Snopes reported that city officials and police had no evidence for the pet and wildlife claims and that some evidence used online came from outside Springfield.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Social media posts claimed Haitian immigrants in Springfield were eating cats, ducks, geese and other pets.
nps_use_note: Highly salient seed for studying group-threat and meme-driven narrative distortion.

original_verified_report: During the Sept. 10 presidential debate, local officials had already rebutted the claim that Haitian immigrants in Springfield were eating pets.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited Springfield's mayor, city spokesperson and police, all saying the claims were unfounded and based on unverified social media posts.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Donald Trump said in the debate that immigrants in Springfield were eating dogs, cats and residents' pets.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether elite repetition overwhelms verified local evidence.

election_context: Ohio official response to Springfield rumors
original_verified_report: Ohio Gov. Mike DeWine said Haitian immigrants in Springfield were legally present and that pet-eating claims were garbage with no evidence.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: Politico reported DeWine's comments that Haitians in Springfield were legal, had a right to be there and were contributing to local employers despite real service-capacity strains.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Political rhetoric continued to frame Springfield's Haitian community as illegal, chaotic and dangerous despite state-level correction.
nps_use_note: Good for studying whether corrective elite cues from the same party reduce distortion.

original_verified_report: Springfield officials said claims that Haitian migrants were eating pets were unfounded.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated Bernie Moreno's pet-eating narrative Pants on Fire, citing repeated official rebuttals from Springfield.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Bernie Moreno said illegal Haitians were sucking up services and reportedly killing and eating pets.
nps_use_note: Useful for observing how local misinformation is converted into campaign blame.

original_verified_report: Recent false claims about immigrants and crime frequently relied on isolated incidents, unfounded rumors or distorted statistics rather than evidence of higher immigrant crime rates.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reviewed multiple immigration-crime claims, including the Springfield rumor, and described how they created misleading impressions about immigrants and criminality.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Online and campaign narratives framed immigrants broadly as a crime wave despite weak or false evidence.
nps_use_note: Useful aggregate seed for testing whether agents move from individual cases to group blame.

election_context: Haitian legal status in Springfield discussion
original_verified_report: Haitians who entered through humanitarian parole or held Temporary Protected Status had temporary legal authorization to live and work in the U.S.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited immigration lawyers and USCIS information explaining that humanitarian parole and TPS are lawful temporary statuses, even if the Trump administration later sought to end them.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: JD Vance and Trump officials framed beneficiaries of parole and TPS programs as illegal immigrants because they disagreed with the legality of the programs.
nps_use_note: Good for testing whether agents preserve formal legal status under partisan pressure.

original_verified_report: Trump's 2024 campaign repeatedly used unsupported or misleading immigration claims about prisons, crime, voting, jobs and Social Security.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact summarized eleven repeated immigration falsehoods from Trump's campaign and provided corrections for each claim category.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The campaign narrative treated immigration as a common cause of crime, job loss, election fraud and welfare collapse.
nps_use_note: Useful as a broad seed for studying how separate claims merge into one immigration-threat master narrative.

original_verified_report: Trump's second administration began reinstating Remain in Mexico, expanding border enforcement and seeking to narrow legal immigration pathways.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's immigration promise tracker noted that Trump began the process for reinstating Remain in Mexico on Jan. 20, 2025.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Supporters framed these actions as restoring control from an invasion, while critics framed them as dismantling legal migration and asylum protections.
nps_use_note: Useful for simulation of policy action becoming competing legitimacy narratives.

original_verified_report: A federal judge temporarily blocked Trump's executive order seeking to end birthright citizenship, and litigation continued.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's first-100-days immigration analysis cited the blocked birthright-citizenship order as part of the administration's effort to reshape legal immigration categories.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Public debate framed birthright citizenship alternately as constitutional bedrock, birth tourism abuse, or a loophole rewarding illegal entry.
nps_use_note: Useful for studying constitutional meaning drift around immigration policy.

election_context: Immigration and voting narrative at CPAC
original_verified_report: There is no evidence that Biden's immigration policy was a giant voter importation scam; noncitizens cannot vote in federal elections and naturalization takes years.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact cited removals and expulsions data, citizenship requirements and experts rejecting the voter-importation claim.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Elon Musk said Biden's immigration policy was a giant voter importation scam.
nps_use_note: Useful for tracking how immigration and election legitimacy narratives fuse.

original_verified_report: Trump struck deals and set new policies to advance his mass deportation promise, but implementation depends on legal, diplomatic and logistical constraints.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's immigration analysis linked the administration's first-100-days actions to campaign promises while noting court challenges and legal pathway disputes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Political narratives framed mass deportation either as rapid removal of dangerous criminals or as indiscriminate expulsion of lawful and vulnerable migrants.
nps_use_note: Useful for modeling divergent interpretations of the same enforcement policy.

original_verified_report: Legal experts said Tren de Aragua's criminal activity and illegal immigration did not constitute an invasion under the Alien Enemies Act.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact interviewed legal experts who said invasion in the Alien Enemies Act context refers to armed attacks or warlike incursions, not ordinary immigration or gang crime.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump's proclamation characterized Tren de Aragua as invading the U.S. and used that frame to justify fast deportations.
nps_use_note: Strong seed for testing how legal categories become emergency narratives.

original_verified_report: JD Vance's statement that Kilmar Abrego Garcia was a convicted MS-13 gang member was false; available court records did not show such a conviction.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's first-100-days analysis cited its Abrego Garcia fact-checks and explained that the administration overstated gang and criminal findings.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Vance and allied narratives framed Abrego Garcia as a convicted MS-13 member to justify deportation to El Salvador.
nps_use_note: Useful for studying how legal uncertainty becomes categorical criminal identity.

country_region: United States - El Salvador deportation dispute
election_context: Bukele and Trump officials on Abrego Garcia
original_verified_report: Officials' statements about Abrego Garcia's gang status, immigration protection and return from El Salvador required context or were inaccurate.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained that bond rulings are not criminal convictions, withholding of removal protected Abrego Garcia from removal to El Salvador, and designation of MS-13 did not automatically revoke his protections.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Officials portrayed Abrego Garcia's deportation as straightforward removal of a gang member despite a court protection against return to El Salvador.
nps_use_note: Good for omission-sensitive narrative distortion in legal cases.

original_verified_report: A photo promoted as showing MS-13 lettering on Abrego Garcia's knuckles appeared altered; gang experts said the visible symbols were not known MS-13 markers.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact compared other photos of Abrego Garcia's hand and interviewed gang experts, finding the asserted MS-13 lettering absent elsewhere and unsupported.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump and the White House promoted the hand image as proof of MS-13 membership.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing visual-source trust and criminal identity propagation.

original_verified_report: Trump's first-100-days immigration agenda drew on narratives that migrants were invading, increasing crime and being sent from foreign prisons, although these narratives were false or misleading.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact interviewed lawyers, historians and criminologists and reviewed claims behind Alien Enemies Act deportations, legal pathway terminations and court-authority disputes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Administration messaging framed immigration policies as responses to invasion, criminal importation and judicial overreach.
nps_use_note: High-level seed for testing how policy bundles inherit narrative premises.

original_verified_report: There was no evidence that ICE deportation flights were dropping immigrants in the middle of the ocean.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that an immigration activist who tracks ICE flights called the claim baseless and that no evidence supported bodies washing ashore from such flights.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: TikTok posts claimed ICE planes flew people into the ocean and returned, with bodies washing up cuffed and shackled.
nps_use_note: Useful for comparing anti-ICE extreme atrocity narratives with verified enforcement criticism.

election_context: Abrego Garcia criminal allegations after return
original_verified_report: Trump administration claims about Kilmar Abrego Garcia's gang membership, trafficking and abuse history were mixed, exaggerated or based on disputed evidence.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that recent judicial decisions said the government had not proven gang membership and that Abrego Garcia was charged with human smuggling, not human trafficking.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Officials described him as an MS-13 gang member, human trafficker, serial domestic abuser and child predator.
nps_use_note: Useful for measuring cumulative label stacking in immigration enforcement narratives.

original_verified_report: A 1.6 million decrease estimate for immigrants in the U.S. illegally had major caveats and did not prove all had gone home voluntarily.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained the estimate used a survey with small sample size and large margin of error and included deportations, deaths, voluntary departures and status changes.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Kristi Noem said Bureau of Labor Statistics figures showed 1.6 million illegal aliens had gone home voluntarily.
nps_use_note: Good for testing whether agents retain caveats about uncertain migration estimates.

original_verified_report: A federal appeals court ruled Trump could not use the Alien Enemies Act to deport Venezuelan gang members in the states within its jurisdiction, while due-process questions continued.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained the Fifth Circuit decision, due-process requirements and continuing litigation over notice and deportation authority.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The White House framed the ruling as courts interfering with national security and the president's sole authority to remove terrorists.
nps_use_note: Useful for studying separation-of-powers distortion around immigration.

original_verified_report: ICE officers have legal protections while performing duties, but Stephen Miller's sweeping statement that all ICE officers have federal immunity was too broad and needed context.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed Miller's Oct. 24, 2025 statement as Mostly False, indicating the claim overstated legal immunity for ICE officers.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Miller told ICE officers they had federal immunity in the conduct of their duties.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing whether agents transform legal protection into unchecked power.

original_verified_report: Housing costs are shaped by supply shortages and other factors; the claim that expensive housing resulted from flooding the country with 30 million illegal immigrants overstated immigration's role and used an unsupported population figure.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed JD Vance's Nov. 13, 2025 housing claim as Mostly False in its immigration fact-checks.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: JD Vance said housing is expensive because the country was flooded with 30 million illegal immigrants and because of a housing shortage.
nps_use_note: Good for studying how economic scarcity is narratively assigned to immigrants.

election_context: Border Patrol operation in North Carolina
original_verified_report: The Trump administration deployed Border Patrol agents in North Carolina's largest cities, triggering claims from multiple sides about arrests, crime and community effects.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact fact-checked claims surrounding the North Carolina crackdown after protests and public dispute over federal immigration enforcement.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Narratives alternately framed the operation as removing criminals from the streets or terrorizing immigrant communities.
nps_use_note: Useful for agent simulations with partisan roles interpreting the same enforcement event.

election_context: ICE deportation of brothers with long check-ins
original_verified_report: Two Salvadoran brothers were deported after years of immigration check-ins and good conduct, complicating claims that enforcement targeted only the worst of the worst.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact's Year of the Lies article documented the brothers' history of check-ins and deportation and contrasted it with administration rhetoric about prioritizing serious criminals.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Administration rhetoric described deportees broadly as the worst of the worst, while critics framed the case as evidence of indiscriminate deportation.
nps_use_note: Good for testing whether individual cases shift perceived legitimacy of mass enforcement.

original_verified_report: There was no evidence that Nicolás Maduro was releasing prisoners and sending them to the U.S. southern border under Biden.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated Ron DeSantis' claim false and repeated that earlier Venezuelan prisoner-release narratives lacked evidence.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Ron DeSantis said Maduro was releasing people from prisons and sending them to the southern border.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing persistence of debunked foreign-prison narratives into 2026.

original_verified_report: Viral photos and images after a Jan. 7 Minneapolis immigration-enforcement shooting misidentified people involved in the incident.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed Jan. 7, 2026 social media claims about the officer and the woman killed as false or Pants on Fire.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Social posts falsely identified unmasked men or unrelated photos as the ICE officer or the woman killed.
nps_use_note: Good for testing visual rumor propagation after enforcement violence.

original_verified_report: Mark Warner's statement that ICE agents were not getting the traditional five months of training and that training was now 47 days was mostly true.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed Warner's Jan. 11, 2026 English and Spanish claims about ICE training as Mostly True.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Critics framed shortened ICE training as proof of reckless enforcement; defenders framed the claim as an attack on law enforcement.
nps_use_note: Useful for simulating how administrative facts become public-safety narratives.

original_verified_report: There was no evidence that anti-ICE protesters in Minnesota were fake professional agitators or paid insurrectionists.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact reported that critics of the protests falsely called protesters paid and that available evidence did not support Trump's claim.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump said protesters against the Minnesota immigration crackdown were fake protests by agitators and professional insurrectionists.
nps_use_note: Useful for tracking how dissent is reframed as illegitimate orchestration.

original_verified_report: JD Vance was wrong that Minneapolis was the place with the highest concentration of people who violated immigration laws.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated Vance's statement false and explained that Minneapolis did not rank No. 1 in unauthorized immigrants.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Vance justified the Minneapolis focus by saying it had the highest concentration of immigration-law violators.
nps_use_note: Good for testing geographic threat inflation.

original_verified_report: An image did not show Alex Pretti holding a gun while pinned by federal immigration agents; PolitiFact found the claim false.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed the Jan. 25, 2026 X claim that Pretti held a gun, not a phone, as false.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: X posts claimed Pretti had a gun rather than a phone during an immigration-enforcement encounter.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing how object ambiguity shifts blame and perceived threat.

original_verified_report: Evan Kilgore was not one of the Border Patrol agents who shot Alex Pretti in Minneapolis.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed the Jan. 25, 2026 social media claim naming Kilgore as a shooter as Pants on Fire.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Social media users falsely named Evan Kilgore as one of the agents involved in the shooting.
nps_use_note: Useful for examining doxxing-like narrative drift after enforcement incidents.

original_verified_report: Donald Trump was wrong that 92% of Minnesota Somalis do not work.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated the claim Pants on Fire and cited labor and demographic data contradicting the assertion.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump said among Somalis in Minnesota, 92% do not work.
nps_use_note: Strong seed for measuring group-level economic stereotyping.

original_verified_report: Trump's claim that Minneapolis crime was down 25% to 30% because thousands of criminals had been removed was only barely true and overstated causal certainty.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed the Feb. 8, 2026 NBC claim as Mostly False, indicating the causal claim and numbers needed substantial context.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Trump attributed a large crime drop in Minneapolis to removing thousands of criminals through immigration enforcement.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing causal attribution drift.

original_verified_report: Bad Bunny did not bring Liam Conejo Ramos, a 5-year-old detained by ICE in Minneapolis, onto the Super Bowl halftime stage or give him a Grammy during the performance.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed English and Spanish Feb. 8, 2026 social media claims about Bad Bunny and Liam Conejo Ramos as false.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Social posts claimed Bad Bunny used the Super Bowl stage to spotlight or honor the detained child.
nps_use_note: Good for studying how celebrity events are recruited into immigration narratives.

original_verified_report: Biden did not reverse a policy of deporting people in the country illegally who committed felonies; his administration set enforcement priorities but did not categorically stop felony deportations.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact explained Biden's 2021 enforcement priorities and deportation pause context and rated Ashley Moody's claim false.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Ashley Moody said Biden reversed a policy of deporting immigrants who committed felonies.
nps_use_note: Useful for testing nuanced enforcement-priority interpretation.

original_verified_report: A video claiming to show an ICE agent removing a U.S. citizen from a plane for being Latino was a skit, not a real incident.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found the Facebook video was staged even though ICE agents are present at some airports.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Facebook posts presented the staged video as a real case of ICE removing a U.S. citizen for being Latino.
nps_use_note: Useful for comparing real civil-liberties concerns with fabricated incident evidence.

original_verified_report: Calling the Dignity Act mass amnesty was only half true; the bill included legal status and possible citizenship pathways along with requirements and enforcement provisions.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact rated the claim Half True because the legislation included legalization elements but the mass amnesty label omitted fees, work requirements and enforcement components.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Brandon Gill said the Dignity Act is mass amnesty.
nps_use_note: Good for testing whether agents preserve multi-part policy design.

original_verified_report: An ad saying Seth Moulton thanked ICE as agents were terrorizing communities and killed citizens in broad daylight was rated barely true and needed significant context.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact listed the Commonwealth Together PAC ad as Mostly False in its immigration fact-checks, noting the vote and claim required context.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: The ad connected Moulton's vote thanking ICE to claims of terrorizing communities and citizens killed in broad daylight.
nps_use_note: Useful for observing campaign-ad blame reassignment around ICE.

original_verified_report: There was no evidence that 950 immigrants collapsed part of the U.S.-Mexico border wall in April 2026; experts said the viral image was probably AI-generated.
supporting_fact_check_evidence: PolitiFact found no credible news reports of the event and identified visual irregularities consistent with AI generation.
real_world_distorted_or_reframed_variant: Instagram and Facebook posts claimed 950 immigrants knocked down the border wall trying to enter the U.S.
nps_use_note: Strong seed for testing visual-AI immigration panic narratives.
